---
title: Time-series Forecasting in Safety-critical Environments
authors:
  - name: Thomas Bartz-Beielstein
    affiliations:
      - name: Bartz & Bartz GmbH, 51643 Gummersbach, Germany
    orcid: 0000-0002-5938-5158
    email: bartzbeielstein@gmail.com
    url: https://www.spotseven.de
    corresponding: true
    roles: writing, conceptualization, supervision
  - name: Eva Bartz
    affiliation: Bartz & Bartz GmbH, 51643 Gummersbach, Germany
    orcid: 0009-0005-2989-2385
    email: eva.bartz@bartzundbartz.de
    roles: writing
runninghead: "spotforecast2-safe"
abstract: |
    A current survey of the Python open-source packages available for time-series forecasting finds that none of them ships an end-to-end evidence trail that would withstand audits under Articles 10, 13, and 15 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 on Artificial Intelligence (hereinafter referred to as the "EU AI Act"), under IEC 61508 (functional safety), or under the ISA/IEC 62443 series for cybersecurity in industrial automation and control systems. The Python package `spotforecast2-safe` fills this gap. It is intended for deployment in safety-critical environments. The package presented in this report is based on the open-source package `skforecast`. It honours four non-negotiable code-level engineering rules: zero dead code (every line of code is exercised), deterministic processing steps, fail-safe processing (invalid input is not silently repaired), and a minimal dependency footprint.
    The process-level rules that accompany these code-level rules are implemented through model cards, in-code documentation (executable docstrings), continuous-integration workflows, a Common Platform Enumeration (CPE) identifier, REUSE-compliant licensing, and the release pipeline. Plotting, AutoML, and deep-learning (or large-language-model) backends are deliberately excluded because each of these components either inflates the attack surface, introduces nondeterminism, or breaks reproducibility. This report documents the architecture, formalises the preprocessing and forecasting algorithms, maps the design decisions onto the transparency and robustness articles of the EU AI Act, and contains a worked end-to-end example for forecasting electricity generation, transmission, and consumption on the European market using ENTSO-E Transparency Platform data. The package is open source under AGPL-3.0-or-later.

    \textbf{Keywords:} Time-series forecasting, Safety-critical machine learning, EU AI Act, Deterministic computing, skforecast, spotforecast2
jupyter: spotforecast2-safe
echo: true
reference-section-title: References
header-includes: |
  \usepackage{dirtree}
  \DefineVerbatimEnvironment{Highlighting}{Verbatim}{commandchars=\\\{\},fontsize=\small}
format:
  arxiv-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    mainfont: "TeX Gyre Termes"
    mathfont: "TeX Gyre Termes Math"
    number-sections: true
    toc: false
    classoption:
      - onecolumn
---

# Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Software-engineering practice in Artificial Intelligence (AI)^[In what follows we use AI as an umbrella term for machine learning, deep-neural-network learning, and language models.] and software engineering in safety-critical applications have until recently occupied largely disjoint software ecosystems. AI-based software development for time-series forecasting favours feature-rich libraries that integrate plotting, hyperparameter search, probabilistic deep-learning backends, and interactive dashboards into a single installable package. These feature-rich libraries are excellent for exploratory work and for innovative research (and publication). This richness, however, becomes a liability once the resulting forecasting model is moved into an environment that must withstand an audit for safety-critical systems. Plotting tools, for example, require a sizeable number of graphics libraries to be pulled in. The same applies to the AutoML layer and to deep-learning models, which additionally tend to have very short release cycles, so that the long-term reproducibility that regulators ask about is not easy to guarantee.

A concrete scan of the 2026 Python forecasting landscape confirms this tension. The three dominant abstractions --- the unified `Forecaster` / `BaseEstimator` API of `sktime` [@loni19a; @loni20a], the multi-backend wrapper of `Darts` [@herz22a], and the recursive and direct multi-step forecasters of `skforecast` [@scip24a] --- all ship plotting (via `matplotlib` or `plotly`), AutoML search (via `optuna`), and optional deep-learning backends (via `torch` or `tensorflow`). The Nixtla stack [@garz22a] improves runtime throughput but entails substantial package dependencies, including `torch`.
The classical references (`statsmodels`, `pmdarima`) are leaner, but neither offers a dedicated fail-safe semantics for invalid input nor a pinned CPE identifier for vulnerability tracking. Compliance-monitoring libraries such as `Deepchecks` [@chor22a] and `Evidently AI`^[\url{https://github.com/evidentlyai/evidently}] cover the *evaluation* side of the robustness requirement in Art. 15 of the EU AI Act, but they sit on top of an already-finished forecasting package (forecasting library) and do not change the provenance, determinism, or dependency footprint of the software itself.
As a consequence, a deployer who has to provide the evidence required by Art. 10, 13, and 15 of the EU AI Act, by IEC 61508-3 §7.4.4 (software design and development), by ISO 26262-6 §9 (software unit implementation), or by ISA/IEC 62443-4-1 (Secure Product Development Lifecycle) inherits none of that evidence from the forecasting library itself.
That evidence must, in some cases, be reconstructed at considerable cost.
The software `spotforecast2-safe` presented here moves this evidence into the library, so that the deployer inherits it rather than reconstructing it.

Articles 10, 13, and 15 of the EU AI Act [@euAI24] oblige high-risk AI systems to provide evidence of data-governance practices, transparency toward downstream deployers, and an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity.
A demonstrator that rests on a software stack with dozens of unpinned dependencies is not in principle uncertifiable; the cost of establishing the mandatory audit trail, however, grows with every dependency.
The pre-existing functional-safety standards^[IEC 61508-3 for electrical and electronic systems [@iec61508] and the road-vehicle-specific adaptation ISO 26262-6 [@iso26262].] make the same point from the opposite direction: software whose behaviour depends on uncontrolled externalities cannot be classified beyond Safety Integrity Level (SIL) 1 or Automotive Safety Integrity Level (ASIL) A without a disproportionate verification effort.

Functional safety and AI transparency are, however, not the only regulatory vectors.
In industrial deployment contexts a forecasting library runs as a software component at Level 2 or 3 of the Purdue reference model^[A reference model for automation and industrial networks.] (ISA-95 / IEC 62264), where the dominant cybersecurity framework is the ISA/IEC 62443 series [@iec62443_1_1]. The series partitions responsibility across asset owners (62443-2-1 [@iec62443_2_1]), service providers (62443-2-4 [@iec62443_2_4]), system integrators (62443-3-2 [@iec62443_3_2], 62443-3-3 [@iec62443_3_3]), and component manufacturers (62443-4-1 [@iec62443_4_1], 62443-4-2 [@iec62443_4_2]); the last two parts are directly applicable to a software supplier. Within the European Union (EU), the 62443 series is additionally the leading candidate for a harmonised standard under the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA) [@euCRA24] and informs the risk-management obligations under Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (the NIS2 Directive) [@euNIS22] and the Critical Entities Resilience (CER) Directive [@euRCE22].
@fluc24a describes 62443-2-1 as "the equivalent of ISO/IEC 27001 for cyber-physical systems (CPS)". The part most directly binding on `spotforecast2-safe` is, however, 62443-4-1, whose Secure Product Development Lifecycle requirements map closely onto the four engineering rules of the present package. NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3 [@nist80082r3] provides a complementary US-federal reference with substantial technical overlap.

The package `spotforecast2-safe` is a response to this tension between software engineering in AI research and in safety-critical applications.
The starting point is `skforecast` [@scip24a], a well-documented forecasting library that implements the recursive multi-step approach, lag and window features, direct multi-output forecasting, and rolling-origin cross-validation.
Approximately one third of the safety-critical lines of code are a direct port from the `skforecast` source, with the original BSD-3-Clause copyright notice retained.
 Additional code enforces four code-level engineering rules.
 First, "no dead code"^["Dead code" is code whose removal does not change the program's behaviour.].
 Second, deterministic transformations, including no generation of random numbers without a seed and no reliance on Python dictionary iteration order.
 Third, fail-safe processing, such that NaN values, incorrect dtypes, or missing indices raise explicit exceptions of type `ValueError` or `TypeError`.
 Fourth, minimal footprint, i.e., a version-controlled blocklist ("blocklist") of prohibited dependencies (see @sec-principles) prevents the import of plotting, AutoML, and deep-learning libraries.

 In addition to the code-level rules, four process-level rules are defined.
Observing these rules reduces the feature surface of the software. To compensate for this deliberate reduction, `spotforecast2-safe` is distributed as the subset of a more comprehensive sibling package, `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2]. The `spotforecast2` package adds further features: interactive plotting based on `plotly` and `matplotlib`, AutoML hyperparameter search via `optuna` and `spotoptim`, and model-explanation tooling based on `shap`.

The remainder of this report describes the subset implemented by `spotforecast2-safe`. The report pursues three goals. First, it positions the package within the landscape of Python time-series tools and explains which features are deliberately absent from the safety-critical subset and why (@sec-related). Second, it documents the architecture, the formal preprocessing and forecasting algorithms, and the safety-relevant engineering decisions shipped in the most recent releases (@sec-principles through @sec-safety). Third, it maps the design decisions onto specific provisions of the EU AI Act and of the two functional-safety standards, and closes with a short worked example of electricity-load forecasting (@sec-compliance and @sec-example).
The report is itself part of the audit narrative: every code block is executed at document-render time, and the entire document, including its bibliography, is under version control.

# Related Work {#sec-related}

The Python time-series-forecasting ecosystem has grown substantially over the past decade and has adopted implementations from the statistical programming language R [@r21a]. The scientific baseline is set by `statsmodels`, which provides ARIMA, SARIMAX, and exponential-smoothing models following the canonical treatment of @box15a. `scikit-learn` [@pedr11a] deliberately omits time-series-specific functionality and delegates it to downstream libraries. The main libraries are briefly introduced below.

`skforecast` [@scip24a] provides a `Forecaster` abstraction that wraps any scikit-learn-compatible regressor into a recursive, direct, or multi-series forecaster. It is the closest conceptual relative of the package documented here. We adopt from it the recursive-forecasting architecture, the rolling-origin splitter, and substantial parts of the `_binner` and `_differentiator` modules as well as the residuals infrastructure. We do not adopt the plotting subsystem (which depends on `matplotlib` and `plotly`) or the AutoML-style `bayesian_search_forecaster` (which depends on `optuna` and introduces nondeterministic optimisation traces).
These tools are available in the `spotforecast2` package.

The Nixtla ecosystem implements three complementary libraries: `statsforecast` [@garz22a] provides vectorised implementations of classical models, trading model flexibility for aggressive runtime optimisation via Numba [@lam15a]. `mlforecast` extends the same API to gradient-boosting methods. `neuralforecast` offers deep-learning architectures via a unified interface. The deep-learning backend violates the minimal-footprint rule directly.

`Darts` [@herz22a] introduces a unified API for a variety of time-series models. Its installation footprint is dominated by optional dependencies whose behaviour influences the availability of model classes at import time. The same holds for `tsai`, `Merlion`, and `Kats` [@ogui23a; @bhat21a; @jian22a].

The "M-competitions" [@makr20a; @makr22a] have become the de facto benchmark for point and interval forecasting. Models such as LightGBM [@ke17a] and XGBoost [@chen16a], as well as their ensembles, have established themselves as strong baselines across application domains.
The decision to restrict the package's supported backends to LightGBM, XGBoost, and scikit-learn regressors (and to ship `ForecasterRecursiveLGBM` and `ForecasterRecursiveXGB` as the two default models) follows directly from this empirical evidence.

None of the libraries mentioned is poorly engineered. On the contrary, they represent outstanding (research) software.
However, software optimised for rapid exploration cannot at the same time meet a SIL-3 software-verification budget without extensive deployer-specific rework. `spotforecast2-safe` moves that rework into the package itself, so that the deployer inherits the evidence rather than having to reproduce it.

A parallel line of research addresses *how* the trustworthiness of a high-risk AI system should be assessed. @stet24a propose a trustworthiness-assurance framework that decomposes the EU AI Act requirements into verifiable properties along the V-model and connects AI-system engineering to the assurance argumentation of functional safety. The present report is compatible with that framework: each process-level rule in @sec-principles is an argumentation step, and the *Mechanism* and *Evidence* columns of @tbl-compliance are the substantiations. The legal background is worked through by @raue25a, whose volume on *Artificial Intelligence and Fundamental Rights* analyses the Regulation's interaction with the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, non-discrimination law, and data-protection law. @raue25a is the authoritative companion reference when a deployer has to translate the technical evidence documented here into a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA) under Art. 27 of the EU AI Act.


# Design Principles {#sec-principles}

The design principles separate into two parallel categories. *Code-level engineering rules* constrain what the source code may contain; they are enforced at commit time by a test or by a linter^[A linter is a static-analysis tool that inspects source code without executing it, flagging style violations, suspicious patterns, and deviations from coding conventions. In the present package `ruff`, `black`, `isort`, and `mypy` are invoked via pre-commit hooks.]. *Process-level rules* constrain how the package is developed, released, and operated, and are satisfied through `MODEL_CARD.md`, CI workflows, and the release pipeline, rather than through source-code tests. Both categories are necessary: the code-level rules alone do not satisfy the regulatory obligations for traceability, threat modelling, data governance, supply-chain integrity, or operational observability.

## Code-level engineering rules

The four non-negotiable code-level rules are:

1. *No dead code.* No function, class, or branch is shipped without a test or an executable docstring example reaching it. A `test_docstring_examples_*.py` test suite enforces this.
2. *Deterministic transformations.* Same input, same bit-level output. Random-number generators are seeded; Python dictionary iteration order is not relied upon; `joblib` parallelism is pinned to a fixed `n_jobs` argument.
3. *Fail-safe processing.* Invalid input raises an explicit exception rather than being silently imputed or coerced. Silent imputation is available only behind a typed `Literal["raise", "ffill_bfill", "passthrough"]` opt-in flag that must be set explicitly.
4. *Minimal Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) attack surface.* A short, version-controlled blocklist of prohibited dependencies (`plotly`, `matplotlib`, `spotoptim`, `optuna`, `torch`, `tensorflow`) is enforced by a test that greps the lockfile. Runtime dependencies are restricted to a curated subset of widely used packages whose maintainers publish security advisories.

These rules also recover the secure-development practices required by IEC 62443-4-1 [@iec62443_4_1]. The standard defines eight process areas^[Security Management (SM), Specification of Security Requirements (SR), Secure by Design (SD), Secure Implementation (SI), Security Verification and Validation (SVV), Management of Security-related Issues (DM), Security Update Management (SUM), and Security Guidelines (SG).] at four maturity levels (Maturity Level, ML 1 *Initial* through ML 4 *Improving*). The "no dead code" rule instantiates SM-1 (security-management process) and SI-1 (enforced coding guidelines); "deterministic transformations" instantiates SVV-1 and SVV-3 (reproducible verification); "fail-safe processing" instantiates SR-2 (threat-model-driven requirements) and DM-1 (defect identification); and the minimal-footprint rule instantiates SM-7 (security of third-party components). The Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) maturity level that the package currently evidences is ML 2 *Managed*. ML 3 *Defined* presupposes a formal threat model, which is listed as future work in @sec-outlook.

## Process-level rules

Four further rules govern how the package is developed, released, and operated. None of them can be enforced by a pytest assertion against the source code; each is satisfied by an artefact (`MODEL_CARD.md`, a CI workflow, the semantic-release pipeline, or a logging schema) and checked at review time or at release time. This decomposition mirrors the V-model-based trustworthiness-assurance structure proposed by @stet24a, in which AI-specific regulatory requirements are refined into verifiable properties whose evidence is collected along the development lifecycle.

1. *Traceability.* Every public symbol and every non-trivial private helper links back to a documented requirement in `MODEL_CARD.md` (2. through 5.) or to a specific clause of the compliance table in @sec-compliance. This satisfies IEC 61508-3 clause 7.2 (specification of software safety requirements) [@iec61508], ISO 26262-6 clause 7.4.2 (ASIL-dependent requirements derivation) [@iso26262], and Art. 9 of the EU AI Act (risk management across the entire lifecycle) [@euAI24]. Orphan symbols --- those without a requirement link and without a `MODEL_CARD` entry --- are flagged in code review rather than by CI.
2. *Documented threat model.* A STRIDE table^[STRIDE is a threat-classification scheme introduced by Microsoft. The acronym stands for *Spoofing*, *Tampering*, *Repudiation*, *Information Disclosure*, *Denial of Service*, and *Elevation of Privilege*. A STRIDE table enumerates, per module and per data flow, which of these six categories apply, which countermeasure applies, and in which source file it is implemented.] is maintained for every network-facing module (`downloader/entsoe.py`, `weather/weather_client.py`); any change to the attack surface requires an updated threat entry in the same pull request. This satisfies IEC 62443-4-1 SR-1 and SR-2 (threat-model-driven security requirements) [@iec62443_4_1] and closes the ML-2 $\rightarrow$ ML-3 gap admitted in @sec-outlook.
3. *Signed supply chain.* Every release ships a signed PyPI wheel and a signed Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) attestation, both published to the `sigstore` transparency log alongside the GitHub Release. The signed wheel satisfies IEC 62443-4-1 SUM-3 (secure delivery of updates) [@iec62443_4_1] as well as the corresponding delivery-integrity requirement in Annex I Part I of the CRA [@euCRA24]. The signed SBOM attestation satisfies Annex I Part II of the CRA (vulnerability-handling obligations), whose point (1) explicitly requires producing a software bill of materials. The input side of the supply chain is already covered by code-level rule 4.
4. *Structured audit log.* Every operational action emits a log record conforming to a pinned JSON schema under `~/spotforecast2_safe_models/logs/`; the schema is versioned, and any change is a Conventional-Commits `feat!:` subject that cascades to a major version bump. This satisfies IEC 62443-4-2 SAR 6.1 (audit log accessibility) [@iec62443_4_2] and IEC 61508-3 clause 7.4.7 (verification of the integrated software) [@iec61508], both of which require operational traceability beyond development-time tests.

A further, separately managed rule concerns data governance: the package itself ships no training data; every forecaster object, however, records the provenance of the data on which it was fitted (source URL, retrieval timestamp, content hash) in its persisted state. This satisfies the provenance requirement of Art. 10 of the EU AI Act (data and data governance) [@euAI24], which is as far as a library can go without access to the deployer's actual training data. Assessing representativity, bias, and fitness for the intended purpose remains the deployer's responsibility and is documented in the deployer's own `MODEL_CARD.md`. We treat this obligation as a refinement of the traceability rule rather than as a fifth process-level rule, because the mechanism (provenance as a persisted artefact) is a special case of the requirement-to-implementation linkage.

A further principle, not strictly one of the four but a consequence of them, is that every source file carries an SPDX header in the format recommended by REUSE v3.0 [@reuse30]. Ported `skforecast` files carry both the original BSD-3-Clause notice and the added AGPL-3.0-or-later identifier, so that the licence resolution is a dual-licence derivative and not to be read as an attempt to re-licence another project's software.

# Architecture {#sec-architecture}

The `spotforecast2-safe` package is organised in three layers. The lowest layer (`forecaster/`, `preprocessing/`, `model_selection/`, `data/`) contains the estimator wrappers, transformers, and splitters that descend most directly from `skforecast`. A middle layer (`manager/`) bundles an estimator together with an `ExogBuilder`, a configurator, a logger, and a persistence layer into a single unit of deployment. The top layer (`processing/` and `tasks/`) provides end-to-end pipelines that compose the lower two layers into console scripts.

\dirtree{%
.1 src/spotforecast2\_safe/.
.2 forecaster/.
.3 recursive/.
.3 base.py, utils.py, metrics.py.
.2 preprocessing/.
.3 repeating\_basis\_function.py.
.3 \_binner.py, \_differentiator.py.
.3 curate\_data.py, imputation.py.
.3 linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py.
.3 outlier.py, split.py.
.2 model\_selection/.
.3 split\_ts\_cv.py, split\_one\_step.py.
.3 validation.py.
.2 manager/.
.3 models/, configurator/, exo/.
.3 persistence.py, trainer.py, predictor.py.
.2 processing/, tasks/.
.2 downloader/, weather/.
.2 utils/, exceptions.py.
}

The public API is pinned by `__all__` at the top package level and currently contains eleven symbols: `Period`, `RepeatingBasisFunction`, `ExogBuilder`, `LinearlyInterpolateTS`, three `ForecasterRecursive*` model classes, `ConfigEntsoe`, plus the alias `Config` and the two utility members `__version__` and `hello`. Any API-breaking change to these symbols requires a Conventional-Commits `feat!:` subject and triggers a major version bump via semantic-release. Private modules (anything prefixed with an underscore or living under a submodule not re-exported at the top level) are explicitly unstable and exempt from this contract.

Adding a new estimator wrapper therefore takes place in the `manager/models/` directory: the wrapper inherits from `ForecasterRecursiveModel`, which itself orchestrates `fit`, `predict`, `backtest`, and `save`/`load`. The wrapper is responsible solely for declaring the backend regressor and any backend-specific serialisation hooks. The exogenous-feature layer, the rolling-origin validation, and the audit logging are inherited.

## Relation to `spotforecast2` {#sec-architecture-relation}

The package shares its core with the (upstream) sibling package `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2]. @tbl-spotforecast2-diff lists the concrete omissions. Conversely, the safe subset adds four subpackages that are not present in `spotforecast2` (`datasets/`, `downloader/`, `processing/`, and `weather/`), because they codify the ENTSO-E and weather-data ingestion path on which the audit narrative of @sec-safety relies.
The dependency direction is one-way. `spotforecast2-safe` does not import from `spotforecast2`; it is installable and testable on its own. `spotforecast2` re-exports selected public symbols from `spotforecast2-safe` (notably `ConfigEntsoe`), so that a deployer who chooses the upstream package still sees a consistent configuration surface. This arrangement lets the safe subset be released on its own cadence and certified independently, while the upstream package remains a strict superset for more general, non-regulated use.

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\footnotesize
\caption{\label{tbl-spotforecast2-diff}Modules and runtime dependencies present in the upstream `spotforecast2` package but omitted from the safe subset, with the triggering dependency or rationale.}
\begin{tabular}{p{0.30\columnwidth} p{0.20\columnwidth} p{0.40\columnwidth}}
\hline
\textbf{Upstream module or dependency} & \textbf{Trigger} & \textbf{Rationale} \\
\hline
\texttt{manager/}\newline\texttt{plotter.py} & \texttt{plotly} & Interactive plotting is a prohibited dependency; the safe subset emits pandas DataFrames for the caller's plotting library of choice. \\
\texttt{preprocessing/}\newline\texttt{time\_series\_}\newline\texttt{visualization.py} & \texttt{matplotlib}, \texttt{plotly} & As above. \\
\texttt{preprocessing/}\newline\texttt{outlier\_plots.py} & \texttt{matplotlib}, \texttt{plotly} & As above. \\
\texttt{model\_selection/}\newline\texttt{bayesian\_search.py} & \texttt{optuna} & Bayesian optimisation introduces run-to-run variation that complicates reproducibility claims. \\
\texttt{model\_selection/}\newline\texttt{spotoptim\_search.py} & \texttt{spotoptim} & Same rationale; surrogate-model-based hyperparameter search is out of scope. \\
\texttt{model\_selection/}\newline\texttt{grid\_search.py},\newline\texttt{random\_search.py} & Footprint & Exhaustive and random search are deterministic but would enlarge the \texttt{manager/} surface without a corresponding safety gain. \\
\texttt{manager/}\newline\texttt{models/}\newline\texttt{forecaster\_recursive\_}\newline\texttt{model\_full.py} & \texttt{shap} & Model-explanation output is not required by the audit trail and is not bit-reproducible across \texttt{shap} minor versions. \\
\texttt{manager/}\newline\texttt{models/}\newline\texttt{forecaster\_recursive\_}\newline\texttt{xgb\_full.py} & Extended features & The safe subset ships a lean \texttt{ForecasterRecursiveXGB} without the explanation hooks. \\
\texttt{manager/}\newline\texttt{multitask/}\newline\texttt{spotoptim.py} & \texttt{spotoptim} & Multi-objective hyperparameter search is out of scope. \\
\texttt{stats/} subpackage & Determinism & Analytical utilities whose cross-platform determinism has not yet been verified. \\
\texttt{jupyter}, \texttt{ipykernel} runtime deps & Footprint & Moved to the development-only dependency group. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

# Preprocessing and Features {#sec-preprocessing}

The `preprocessing` subpackage contains four kinds of transformers:
Cyclic encoders render periodic calendar features (hour of day, day of week, day of year) into a smooth representation.
Interpolators reconstruct short gaps under an explicit contract. A quantile binner discretises a continuous feature into equal-frequency buckets.
A differentiator applies a finite-difference operator.
All four transformers inherit from `sklearn.base.TransformerMixin` and are therefore composable in a `Pipeline`.

# Recursive Forecasting {#sec-forecasting}

The forecasting layer implements the recursive multi-step strategy: a single scalar one-step-ahead model is trained and then iterated to produce horizons of arbitrary length [@hynd21a]. For a target series $y_t$, lags $\mathcal{L} = \{\ell_1, \ldots, \ell_p\}$, window features $w_t$ (e.g., rolling mean), and an exogenous vector $x_t$, the forecaster learns
$$
\hat y_{t} = f\!\left(y_{t-\ell_1}, \ldots, y_{t-\ell_p},\, w_t,\, x_t\right),
$$
and the $h$-step forecast is obtained by recursion:
$$
\hat y_{t+h} = f\!\left(\tilde y_{t+h-\ell_1}, \ldots, \tilde y_{t+h-\ell_p},\, w_{t+h},\, x_{t+h}\right),
$$
where $\tilde y_{s} = y_s$ for $s \le t$ and $\tilde y_s = \hat y_s$ otherwise.

The advantage of the recursive strategy in a safety-critical context is that the training and evaluation pipelines are coupled by construction: the same feature-matrix builder emits both the training features and the features used at prediction time, so feature-skew bugs are a compile-time rather than a runtime class of error.
Moreover, a separate model can be trained for each individual prediction target, which can yield better accuracy at the cost of $h$-fold the model count.

Prediction intervals are computed by bootstrap resampling of the in-sample residuals.
For a desired coverage $1 - \alpha$, residuals $\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_N$ are drawn with replacement and added to the recursive point forecast; this yields an empirical interval between the $\alpha/2$ and $1 - \alpha/2$ quantiles. This is the same procedure as in `skforecast`; it is deterministic given a seed, and it does not require the regressor to be probabilistic.

The backend regressors shipped with the package are LightGBM [@ke17a] and XGBoost [@chen16a], implemented as `ForecasterRecursiveLGBM` and `ForecasterRecursiveXGB`. Both are strong baselines on M5-type electricity and retail datasets [@makr22a].
Any scikit-learn-compatible estimator can be substituted by instantiating `ForecasterRecursiveModel` directly.

# Model Selection and Backtesting {#sec-modelselection}

A naive application of `sklearn.model_selection.KFold` to a time-indexed series leaks the future into the training set. Time-series-aware splitters address this by constraining the training segment to precede the test segment in time. The package provides two such splitters and a backtesting driver.

`TimeSeriesFold` implements the expanding-window protocol. Let $T_0$ denote `initial_train_size`, $s$ denote `steps`, and $h$ denote the forecast horizon. The splitter yields train/test index pairs of the form $([0,\, T_0 + k \cdot s),\, [T_0 + k \cdot s,\, T_0 + k \cdot s + h))$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$. A `refit` flag controls whether the forecaster is re-trained at every fold or only once at the start. For scenarios where even the refit cost is prohibitive, the `fold_stride` parameter allows non-overlapping folds that still cover a large test region; the refit work is thus amortised over multiple test regions. @fig-folds visualises a typical configuration.

```{python}
#| label: fig-folds
#| fig-cap: "Rolling-origin backtest folds produced by `TimeSeriesFold` (initial train 80, steps 24, six folds). Training spans are rendered in blue; held-out test spans in orange. The training set grows with each fold, while the held-out fold advances by `steps` observations."
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n_total = 80 + 24 * 6
initial_train = 80
steps = 24
n_folds = 6

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.5, 2.8))
for k in range(n_folds):
    train_start, train_end = 0, initial_train + k * steps
    test_start, test_end = train_end, train_end + steps
    ax.barh(k, train_end - train_start, left=train_start, color="#4C72B0", height=0.7)
    ax.barh(k, test_end - test_start, left=test_start, color="#DD8452", height=0.7)
ax.set_yticks(range(n_folds))
ax.set_yticklabels([f"fold {k + 1}" for k in range(n_folds)])
ax.set_xlabel("observation index")
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlim(0, n_total)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

`OneStepAheadFold` is a thin specialisation with `steps=1`, commonly used for evaluating the conditional-mean regressor itself rather than the horizon-$h$ point forecast. `backtesting_forecaster` composes either splitter with a `ForecasterRecursive*` and a list of metrics (`mean_absolute_error`, `mean_squared_error`, `mean_absolute_scaled_error` [@hynd06a]) and returns a pandas DataFrame of per-fold scores plus the concatenated prediction vector. Parallel refit execution is offered via `joblib` and requires the caller to pin `n_jobs`; this is the one place in the package where parallelism is exposed, and the pinning is mandatory because OpenMP scheduling is a known source of cross-platform nondeterminism.

@bergm12a provide the theoretical justification: under standard stationarity assumptions, the forward-chaining cross-validation estimator is consistent for the one-step mean-squared prediction error, whereas ordinary $k$-fold cross-validation is not. The package therefore exposes only the two time-aware splitters; there is no escape hatch from the manager layer to the `sklearn` originals.

# Safety-critical Engineering {#sec-safety}

The four design principles of @sec-principles are not in themselves sufficient; each requires a specific engineering choice and an accompanying test. This discipline --- translating regulatory provisions into implementable, testable software requirements while maintaining bidirectional traceability between each provision and the mechanism that satisfies it --- is known as *legal-requirements engineering* (LRE) [@otto07a; @breau08a]. @tbl-compliance is the LRE traceability matrix for the present package. A systematic overview of the field is given by @kose24a. The pull requests and incidents that motivated the current design are recorded in @sec-changelog.

## Determinism and reproducibility

Determinism is pursued at three levels. At the estimator level, LightGBM and XGBoost are always instantiated with a `random_state` passed through from the wrapper; additionally, the `deterministic=True` and `force_col_wise=True` options are set on LightGBM to eliminate the row-vs-column histogram-construction path difference observed on multi-core hosts. At the feature-engineering level, the exogenous-feature concatenation order is stable across Python versions because `ExogBuilder` stores its components in a list rather than in a dictionary. At the I/O level, a parquet round-trip preserves dtypes but not the DatetimeIndex frequency; the cache reader therefore compares on values, not on frequency, and the dedicated `test_weather_client.py::TestWeatherServiceCache` suite pins this behaviour.

## Fail-safe NaN handling

The `LinearlyInterpolateTS` transformer accepts an `on_missing: Literal["raise", "ffill_bfill", "passthrough"]` keyword with default `"raise"`:

```python
from spotforecast2_safe.preprocessing import LinearlyInterpolateTS
# Raises ValueError if any NaN remains after interpolation:
t = LinearlyInterpolateTS(on_missing="raise")
# Explicit opt-in to endpoint forward/backward fill:
t = LinearlyInterpolateTS(on_missing="ffill_bfill")
# Caller promises to handle NaN downstream:
t = LinearlyInterpolateTS(on_missing="passthrough")
```

An executable docstring test enforces that each `Literal` value produces the documented behaviour.

## Corrupt-cache quarantine

The weather-data client caches API responses as parquet files in a configurable directory. The cache reader catches only `(OSError, ValueError)` --- the two exception types that `pyarrow` raises on a damaged file --- logs a message at the `WARNING` level, and renames the damaged file to `<cache>.corrupt-<epoch>` so that an operator can forensically recover it. A missing cache file is silent and returns `None`, because that is the expected steady state on a cold boot.

## Narrow exception handling at network boundaries

The "resume from last data point" path in the ENTSO-E downloader catches only `(FileNotFoundError, ValueError, IndexError)` --- corresponding to first-run bootstrap, empty interim frame, and the "no filename yet" signal --- and falls back to "seven days ago". Any other exception propagates, in particular a `PermissionError` on the interim directory, which constitutes a real filesystem bug rather than a recoverable state. The `TestDownloadNewDataResumeFallback` suite encodes both the expected fallbacks and the required propagation of unexpected exceptions.

## SPDX and REUSE compliance

Every source file carries an SPDX header. Ported `skforecast` modules (`forecaster/recursive/_forecaster_recursive.py`, `preprocessing/_binner.py`, `preprocessing/_differentiator.py`, `model_selection/split_ts_cv.py`, and a handful of utilities) carry a dual header:

```
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: skforecast team
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2026 bartzbeielstein
# SPDX-License-Identifier: AGPL-3.0-or-later AND BSD-3-Clause
```

`uv run reuse lint` passes on every commit; the `.reuse/dep5` file records the small number of data fixtures whose licence is declared externally.

## CPE identifier for SBOM integration

A CPE 2.3 identifier [@nistir7695] is pinned by `src/spotforecast2_safe/utils/cpe.py` and round-tripped by `tests/test_cpe.py`. This identifier is what downstream SBOM tooling (`syft`, `cyclonedx-py`) reports when the package appears in a software bill of materials, and what vulnerability scanners use to resolve against the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue [@cisa23sbom]. Pinning the identifier makes it a first-class API contract: a breaking change to the CPE string requires a Conventional-Commits `feat!:` subject and therefore surfaces in the CHANGELOG.

# CPS Security Context and ISA/IEC 62443 {#sec-cps}

## The 62443 series at a glance

The ISA/IEC 62443 family is organised in four groups: *General* (Part 1 --- terminology and models [@iec62443_1_1]), *Policies and Procedures* (Part 2 --- programmes for asset owners [@iec62443_2_1] and service providers [@iec62443_2_4]), *System* (Part 3 --- risk assessment [@iec62443_3_2] and system-level security requirements [@iec62443_3_3]), and *Component* (Part 4 --- secure product development [@iec62443_4_1] and component technical requirements [@iec62443_4_2]). A forecasting library is a *software application* in the 62443-4-2 taxonomy (alongside *embedded device*, *host device*, and *network device*); it therefore inherits the Software Application Requirements (SAR) subset of the component catalogue rather than the full embedded-device profile.

## Foundational requirements and security levels

IEC 62443-3-3 and 62443-4-2 share seven Foundational Requirements (FR 1 Identification and Authentication Control, FR 2 Use Control, FR 3 System Integrity, FR 4 Data Confidentiality, FR 5 Restricted Data Flow, FR 6 Timely Response to Events, FR 7 Resource Availability) and four Security Levels (SL 1 protection against casual or coincidental misuse, through SL 4 protection against intentional violation by actors with extended resources). A pure software library cannot on its own satisfy requirements that depend on the surrounding system --- network segmentation (FR 5) and authentication (FR 1) are the clearest examples --- but it can avoid *breaking* them, and this is precisely the posture `spotforecast2-safe` adopts. FR 3 (system integrity) is supported by deterministic transformations, pinned dependencies, and SPDX-tracked provenance; FR 6 (timely response to events) is supported by narrow exception handling and structured logging (@sec-safety); FR 7 (resource availability) is supported by the minimal-footprint rule that caps the transitive-dependency closure. The library thus supports a SL-2 system when it is itself embedded in a SL-2 zone; the security level is a property of the zone, not of the library in isolation.

## Secure product development lifecycle (62443-4-1)

IEC 62443-4-1 builds on Microsoft's Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) [@howa06a] --- the process introduced by Howard and Lipner in which every development phase (requirements, design, implementation, verification, release, response) is paired with an explicit security activity such as threat modelling, static analysis, fuzz testing, and incident response --- and defines eight process areas at four maturity levels. @tbl-62443-4-1 presents the current evidence status for each practice in `spotforecast2-safe`. The package reaches ML 2 *Managed* across all eight practices; ML 3 *Defined* requires a formal threat model and a documented security-assessment report, tracked in the M1--M7 backlog of `REVIEW_FINDINGS.md`.

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\footnotesize
\caption{Current alignment of the package with the eight process areas of IEC 62443-4-1 clause 5.5. The ML columns indicate the evidenced maturity level, not an external certification.\label{tbl-62443-4-1}}
\begin{tabular}{p{0.10\columnwidth} p{0.33\columnwidth} p{0.38\columnwidth} p{0.05\columnwidth}}
\hline
\textbf{Practice} & \textbf{Required evidence} & \textbf{Package evidence} & \textbf{ML} \\
\hline
SM (Security Management) & Documented security roles; inventory of third-party components; personnel guidance. & \texttt{CONTRIBUTING.md}; \texttt{CODEOWNERS}; pinned \texttt{uv.lock}; SPDX headers on every source file. & 2 \\
SR (Security Requirements) & Threat-driven security requirements; typed API contracts. & Fail-safe \texttt{on\_missing} contract; typed \texttt{Literal} flags on preprocessing; \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} Section 5. & 2 \\
SD (Secure by Design) & Defense-in-depth architecture; trust-boundary identification. & Layered architecture (Section~\ref{sec-architecture}); prohibited-dependencies blocklist; narrow exception handling at the network boundary. & 2 \\
SI (Secure Implementation) & Coding guidelines enforced by tooling. & Pre-commit hooks for \texttt{black}, \texttt{isort}, \texttt{ruff}, \texttt{mypy}; SPDX-header checker. & 2 \\
SVV (Security Verification) & Reproducible tests; coverage target; security tests. & Executable docstrings; \texttt{test\_docstring\_examples\_*.py}; $\ge$80\,\% coverage; CodeQL workflow. & 2 \\
DM (Defect Management) & Process for receiving, triaging, and fixing security-related issues. & GitHub Security Advisories; CodeQL and Scorecard workflows; \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}. & 2 \\
SUM (Security Update Management) & Signed update delivery; communication of security advisories. & Semantic-release $\rightarrow$ signed PyPI wheel and GitHub Release; CHANGELOG regeneration. & 2 \\
SG (Security Guidelines) & Operator-facing security documentation. & \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} Sections 4 through 6; the present report. & 2 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

## CPS particularities that shape the design

@fluc19a and @fluc24a highlight four particularities of CPS cybersecurity that distinguish it from classical IT security and that shape the design of the present package:

1. *Long lifecycles.* Industrial plants operate for 20 to 40 years; a forecasting model shipped today may still be producing set-points in the year 2050. Deterministic serialisation, a pinned public API, and a CPE identifier are necessary preconditions for retrospective auditability over that horizon.
2. *Safety-security convergence.* A forecast that drives a physical actuator is a safety-function input. The technical report IEC TR 63069 explicitly addresses the joint safety-and-security problem; the package endorses its conclusion that safety decisions, once made, must not be overturned by a later security fix --- hence the fail-safe default "raise on invalid input" rather than silent data repair.
3. *Late security integration as the dominant failure cause.* Interviews with engineers at INEOS and HIMA reported by @fluc24a show that security is typically bolted on after the Basic Engineering phase, i.e., after the design-time favourable decisions (network topology, component selection) have already been made. A library that exposes explicit error paths and machine-readable provenance is cheaper to integrate early than one that hides state.
4. *Cross-disciplinary authorship.* @fluc24a [REQ 2.2.1] require that security decisions be accessible to non-security specialists. The package's executable docstrings, typed contracts, and file-based audit logs serve exactly this role for the process engineer who inherits a fitted forecaster without being a Python or a security specialist.

The concept underlying item 4 is developed by @fluc22a as a *security decision base*: a structured notation for security-relevant information that satisfies six requirements --- support for human decision-making (R1), accessibility for automation engineers (R2), ICS specificity (R3), methodology independence (R4), traceability of the decision itself (R5), and reusability (R6). Process-level rules 1 (traceability) and 2 (documented threat model) in @sec-principles are the `spotforecast2-safe` instantiation of R5 and R3 for the narrower case of a Python forecasting library.

VDI/VDE 2182-1 [@vdivde2182] provides a procedure model that operationalises these principles in the German industrial-automation context; the standard is cited here because several prospective deployers of `spotforecast2-safe` use it as a checklist during factory acceptance tests.


Art. 4 of the EU AI Act [@euAI24] obliges providers and deployers to ensure that their staff and any other persons operating or using an AI system on their behalf possess a sufficient level of AI literacy, calibrated to the context of use and to the population on which the system acts. `spotforecast2-safe` is designed so that a process engineer without formal machine-learning training can reach that threshold by reading the package itself. Three mechanisms contribute to this. First, every public transformer and forecaster ships with executable docstring examples, so that the intended behaviour can be learned by running small, self-contained snippets rather than by studying external tutorials. Second, typed contracts make the pre- and post-conditions of each call visible at the call site; this reduces the specialist knowledge required to read a training script correctly. Third, the structured audit log (rule 4 in @sec-principles) exposes, line by line, the sequence of decisions that a running forecaster has taken, so that deployer staff can review operational behaviour after the fact. The per-model `MODEL_CARD.md` complements these mechanisms by stating the intended operational envelope and known limitations in language that non-specialist operators can also work with. Taken together, these design decisions lower the AI-literacy threshold that the deployer must reach to supervise the system responsibly and thereby directly support the obligation under Art. 4 of the EU AI Act. The same mechanisms also address the provider-side counterpart in Art. 14(4) of the EU AI Act [@euAI24], under which a high-risk AI system must be delivered in a form that enables the persons assigned to human oversight to understand the capacities and limitations of the system (Art. 14(4)(a) of the EU AI Act), to monitor its operation and detect anomalies, dysfunctions, and unexpected performance (also Art. 14(4)(a)), and to correctly interpret its output (Art. 14(4)(c)). Executable docstrings, typed contracts, and `MODEL_CARD.md` satisfy the understanding and interpretation duties; the structured audit log satisfies the monitoring and anomaly-detection duty. Art. 4 of the EU AI Act establishes the deployer-side literacy obligation and Art. 14(4) establishes the provider-side design obligation that makes this literacy operationally reachable --- the `spotforecast2-safe` artefacts satisfy both obligations from the same source base.


## Relation to the Cyber Resilience Act

The Cyber Resilience Act [@euCRA24], which entered into force on 10 December 2024 and whose main obligations apply from 11 December 2027, imposes essential cybersecurity requirements (Annex I) and a 24-month vulnerability-handling obligation (Annex II) on manufacturers of "products with digital elements". A forecasting library shipped as a wheel on PyPI is a CRA product with digital elements once it is placed on the Union market in the course of a commercial activity. The Commission's Implementing Decision C(2025)618 of 3 February 2025 (standardisation request M/606) tasks CEN, CENELEC, and ETSI with the preparation of 41 harmonised standards; type-C product-category standards are due by 30 October 2026, and type-B technical-measures standards by 30 October 2027. EN IEC 62443-4-1 is among the expected type-B references. Alignment with 62443-4-1 today is therefore also the lowest-cost path to a CRA conformity assessment in 2027. The NIS2 Directive [@euNIS22] is already in force; Art. 21(2)(e) of the NIS2 Directive obliges essential and important entities to adopt "security in the acquisition, development and maintenance of network and information systems" --- the SDL evidence from @tbl-62443-4-1 satisfies the part of that obligation that a software supplier can satisfy on its own.

# Compliance Mapping {#sec-compliance}

The code-level and process-level rules of @sec-principles map onto specific clauses of the EU AI Act, IEC 61508-3, ISO 26262-6, the ISA/IEC 62443 series, and the Cyber Resilience Act. @tbl-compliance records the correspondence. Each row names a clause or requirement, the mechanism in the package that addresses it, and the test or Continuous Integration (CI) workflow in which that mechanism is exercised.

The EU AI Act organises the provider-side obligations around Art. 17; Art. 17 requires a documented Quality Management System (QMS) [@hens24a] that integrates the individual substantive obligations (risk management in Art. 9, data governance in Art. 10, technical documentation in Art. 11, record-keeping obligations in Art. 12, transparency in Art. 13, accuracy and robustness in Art. 15) with the provider-side process obligations (record-keeping by the provider under Art. 18, post-market monitoring under Art. 72, and reporting of serious incidents under Art. 73). @tbl-compliance therefore includes an Art. 17 row whose Mechanism column points to the mechanisms that satisfy each sub-obligation and whose Evidence column cross-references the adjacent rows. A deployer preparing a conformity assessment can read the Art. 17 row as the index into the rest of the table.

Beyond the binding obligations of the Regulation, the AI-specific extension to the *Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation* (SQuaRE) series, ISO/IEC 25059:2023 [@isoiec25059], supplies the quality vocabulary that the forthcoming CEN-CENELEC harmonised standards under Art. 40 of the EU AI Act are expected to adopt. Its quality model refines the functional-correctness, robustness, and user-controllability sub-characteristics of ISO/IEC 25010 for AI systems and is thus the natural technical counterpart to Art. 15 of the EU AI Act. The 2023 edition is currently under revision: the Draft International Standard (DIS) ISO/IEC 25059:2025 is in enquiry, and an EU-AI-Act-tailored European version (preliminary European Norm, prEN ISO/IEC 25059) is expected to enter public enquiry through CEN-CENELEC Joint Technical Committee 21 (JTC 21).

```{=latex}
\begin{table*}
\centering
\small
\caption{Mapping of regulatory clauses to package mechanisms and the evidence that exercises them. EUAIA = Regulation (EU) 2024/1689; IEC61508 = IEC 61508-3:2010; ISO26262 = ISO 26262-6:2018; IEC62443 = ISA/IEC 62443 series; CRA = Regulation (EU) 2024/2847 (Cyber Resilience Act); NIS2 = Directive (EU) 2022/2555.}
\label{tbl-compliance}
\begin{tabular}{p{0.16\textwidth} p{0.44\textwidth} p{0.32\textwidth}}
\hline
\textbf{Clause} & \textbf{Mechanism} & \textbf{Evidence} \\
\hline
EUAIA Art.~9 (risk management across the lifecycle) & The four process-level rules of @sec-principles constitute the risk-management framework; STRIDE threat model per network-facing module; threat entries are updated in the same pull request as the corresponding code change. & \texttt{CONTRIBUTING.md}; per-module threat-model entries; review gate in \texttt{.github/pull\_request\_template.md}. \\
EUAIA Art.~10 (data governance) & Explicit \texttt{on\_missing} contract; no silent imputation; provenance of training data recorded in \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}. & \texttt{tests/test\_linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py} exercises all three \texttt{Literal} values; \texttt{tests/data/test\_fetch\_data.py} covers the I/O contract. \\
EUAIA Art.~11 and Annex~IV (technical documentation) & \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} maintained in the repository covering intended purpose, data provenance, metrics, and limitations; auto-generated API documentation via quartodoc. & \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}; \texttt{docs/} built by \texttt{docs.yml}. \\
EUAIA Art.~12 (record-keeping / automatic logs) & Dual-handler logger: plaintext console, JSON file sink conforming to a pinned schema (\texttt{audit\_log\_schema.json}); \texttt{SCHEMA\_VERSION} is derived from the schema, and schema edits are bound by the CI job \texttt{audit-log-schema-gate} to a Conventional-Commits \texttt{feat!:} subject and cascade to a major version bump via semantic-release. & \texttt{src/spotforecast2\_safe/manager/logger.py}; \texttt{src/spotforecast2\_safe/manager/audit\_log\_schema.json}; \texttt{tests/manager/test\_logger\_schema.py}; \texttt{.github/workflows/ci.yml} job \texttt{audit-log-schema-gate}. \\
EUAIA Art.~13 (transparency) & Executable docstrings for every public symbol; pinned public API in \texttt{\_\_all\_\_}; CHANGELOG generated by semantic-release. & \texttt{test\_docstring\_examples\_*.py}; \texttt{test\_cpe.py}; CHANGELOG.md. \\
EUAIA Art.~15 (accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity) & Deterministic transformations; prohibited-dependencies blocklist; narrow exception handling at network boundaries; CPE identifier for SBOM. & \texttt{test\_exceptions\_examples.py}; \texttt{tests/test\_prohibited\_dependencies.py}; \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}. \\
ISO/IEC 25059:2023 (SQuaRE quality model for AI systems --- technical counterpart to Art.~15 of the EU AI Act) & Functional correctness via deterministic transformations; robustness via typed configuration and the fail-safe \texttt{on\_missing} contract; user controllability via the prohibition on silent auto-tuning documented in @sec-principles. & Aggregates the Art.~15 row evidence: \texttt{test\_exceptions\_examples.py}; \texttt{test\_linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py}; absence of tuning entry points verified by \texttt{grep} in \texttt{ci.yml}. \\
EUAIA Art.~17 (quality management system --- umbrella clause) & QMS documented as: (i) compliance strategy via the process-level rules of @sec-principles; (ii) design control in \texttt{CONTRIBUTING.md}; (iii) verification and validation in the CI workflows; (iv) data management in the Art.~10 row; (v) risk management in the Art.~9 row; (vi) post-market monitoring and incident reporting in the Art.~72 and Art.~73 rows; (vii) record-keeping in the Art.~12 and Art.~18 rows; (viii) resource and accountability framework in \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} Section 5 and \texttt{CODEOWNERS}. & Aggregates the adjacent rows; QMS summary maintained in \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} Section 5; version-controlled under git, enabling reconstruction of the QMS at any release tag. \\
EUAIA Art.~18 (record-keeping by providers --- documentation, logs, conformity records) & The repository itself is the records archive: Conventional-Commits history, semantic-release tags, \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}, per-release \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}, \texttt{uv.lock} snapshot per tag. Records are retained for the ten-year period of Art.~18 of the EU AI Act via git history and GitHub Releases. & \texttt{.releaserc.json}; \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}; \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}; git tags. \\
EUAIA Art.~72 (post-market monitoring) & Structured audit log emitted under \texttt{\~{}/spotforecast2\_safe\_models/logs/}; CPE identifier enables vulnerability tracking post-release. & \texttt{manager/logger.py}; \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}. \\
EUAIA Art.~73 (reporting of serious incidents to authorities) & GitHub Security Advisories as the private-disclosure channel; CodeQL and Scorecard workflows as early-detection signals; CHANGELOG entries reference advisory IDs; public disclosure via GitHub Releases when a fix ships. & \texttt{codeql.yml}, \texttt{scorecard.yml}; \texttt{SECURITY.md}; \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}. \\
IEC 61508-3 clause 7.4.4 (software module testing) & One test file per module; $\ge$80\,\% coverage required for new code; coverage gate in CI. & \texttt{pyproject.toml} \texttt{[tool.coverage]} block; CI workflow \texttt{ci.yml}. \\
IEC 61508-3 clause 7.4.5 (integration testing) & Top-level console-script \texttt{tasks/} exercised end-to-end in CI. & \texttt{uv run spotforecast-safe-demo} in \texttt{ci.yml}. \\
IEC 61508-3 Annex C (software verification) & Executable docstrings; CI blocks merge if docstring examples fail. & \texttt{test\_docstring\_examples\_*.py}. \\
ISO 26262-6 clause 8.4.5 (initialisation of variables) & Fail-safe: uninitialised or NaN inputs raise rather than defaulting to zero. & \texttt{test\_linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py}; \texttt{test\_exog\_builder.py}. \\
ISO 26262-6 clause 9.4.3 (unit testing) & One test file per module; pytest configuration in \texttt{pyproject.toml} in strict mode. & CI workflow \texttt{ci.yml}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 SM-4 (third-party components) & Pinned \texttt{uv.lock}; prohibited-dependencies blocklist; CPE identifier; dual SPDX headers on ported \texttt{skforecast} code. & \texttt{tests/test\_prohibited\_dependencies.py}; \texttt{prohibited-deps} job in \texttt{.github/workflows/ci.yml}; \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}; \texttt{reuse lint}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 SR-2 (threat-model-driven requirements) & Fail-safe \texttt{on\_missing} contract; narrow exception handling at the network boundary. & \texttt{test\_linearly\_interpolate\_ts.py}; \texttt{test\_weather\_client.py}; \texttt{test\_exceptions\_examples.py}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 SVV-3 (security testing) & Executable docstrings; per-module unit tests; $\ge$80\,\% coverage gate; CodeQL. & \texttt{test\_docstring\_examples\_*.py}; \texttt{pyproject.toml [tool.coverage]}; \texttt{codeql.yml}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 DM-1 (management of security-related issues) & GitHub Security Advisories; CodeQL and Scorecard workflows; narrow CVE-relevant exception handling. & \texttt{codeql.yml}, \texttt{scorecard.yml}; \texttt{CHANGELOG.md}. \\
IEC62443-4-1 SUM-3 (delivery of security-related updates) & Semantic-release $\rightarrow$ signed PyPI wheel and GitHub Release; Conventional-Commits gate; CHANGELOG. & \texttt{.releaserc.json}; \texttt{release.yml}. \\
IEC62443-4-2 SAR 3.4 (software integrity) & Deterministic transformations; pinned CPE; parquet round-trip contract. & \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}; \texttt{test\_weather\_client.py::TestWeatherServiceCache}. \\
IEC62443-4-2 SAR 6.1 (audit log accessibility) & Dual-handler logger with file-based audit sink under \texttt{\~{}/spotforecast2\_safe\_models/logs/}. & \texttt{src/spotforecast2\_safe/manager/logger.py}. \\
CRA Annex I No.~1(3)(a) (no known exploitable vulnerabilities) & CPE identifier; CodeQL; Dependabot; \texttt{grep} check of the lockfile against prohibited dependencies. & CI workflows; \texttt{tests/test\_prohibited\_dependencies.py}; \texttt{uv.lock}; \texttt{tests/test\_cpe.py}. \\
CRA Annex I No.~1(3)(b) (secure by default) & Fail-safe default \texttt{on\_missing="raise"}; no implicit network calls at import time. & \texttt{test\_exceptions\_examples.py}. \\
NIS2 Directive Art.~21(2)(e) (security in acquisition, development, maintenance) & SDL evidence from Table~\ref{tbl-62443-4-1}, rolled up in \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md} Section 5. & \texttt{MODEL\_CARD.md}. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}
```

The table does not claim to demonstrate full IEC 61508 SIL-3 certification on the basis of unit tests alone. Such a claim would require integration into a certified hardware/software stack and is outside the scope of an open-source library. The claim is instead that the package eliminates, at source, a specific class of audit findings that would otherwise dominate a regulator's report: undocumented silent imputation, uncontrolled transitive dependencies, nondeterministic output, and untested public API surface.

# Worked Example: Electricity Load Forecasting {#sec-example}

This section demonstrates the full pipeline on a synthetic electricity-load series that emulates the daily and weekly patterns of the ENTSO-E France time zone. Synthetic data is used rather than a live ENTSO-E query [@entsoe24] so that the document render stays deterministic and offline-capable; the `downloader/entsoe.py` module and an Open-Meteo adapter [@openmeteo24] provide the corresponding live-data paths for production use.

```{python}
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=2026)
idx = pd.date_range(
    "2025-01-01", "2025-03-31 23:00", freq="h", tz="UTC"
)
hour = idx.hour.values.astype(float)
dow = idx.dayofweek.values.astype(float)
trend = np.linspace(0, 2.0, len(idx))
daily = 4.0 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * hour / 24 - np.pi / 2)
weekly = 1.5 * (dow < 5).astype(float)  # weekday premium
noise = rng.normal(0, 0.5, size=len(idx))
load = 50.0 + trend + daily + weekly + noise
y = pd.Series(load, index=idx, name="load")
```

The series is split into a training region (first 60 days) and an evaluation region (the remaining 30 days). A `ForecasterRecursiveLGBM` is instantiated with 24 lags, an hour-of-day RBF encoder, and a LightGBM regressor with fixed hyperparameters. We measure the conditional-mean regressor's one-step accuracy on the evaluation region via an expanding-window rolling-origin backtest.

```{python}
#| echo: true
from spotforecast2_safe.preprocessing.repeating_basis_function import (
    RepeatingBasisFunction,
)
from lightgbm import LGBMRegressor

y_train = y.loc["2025-01-01":"2025-03-01"]
y_eval = y.loc["2025-03-02":]
y_all = pd.concat([y_train, y_eval])

# Build an exogenous feature matrix with an hour-of-day RBF encoding
exog_df = pd.DataFrame({"hour": y_all.index.hour.astype(float)}, index=y_all.index)
rbf = RepeatingBasisFunction(n_periods=6, column="hour", input_range=(0, 23))
exog_rbf = pd.DataFrame(
    rbf.fit_transform(exog_df),
    index=exog_df.index,
    columns=[f"rbf_{i}" for i in range(6)],
)

lags = 24
feature_names = [f"lag_{i + 1}" for i in range(lags)] + list(exog_rbf.columns)

X_rows, y_rows = [], []
for t in range(lags, len(y_train)):
    X_rows.append(
        np.concatenate([y_train.values[t - lags : t], exog_rbf.loc[y_train.index[t]].values])
    )
    y_rows.append(y_train.values[t])
X_train = pd.DataFrame(np.vstack(X_rows), columns=feature_names)
y_train_vec = pd.Series(y_rows, name="load")

model = LGBMRegressor(
    n_estimators=200,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    num_leaves=31,
    random_state=2026,
    deterministic=True,
    force_col_wise=True,
    verbose=-1,
)
model.fit(X_train, y_train_vec)

# One-step-ahead prediction over the evaluation region
y_pred = []
history = y_all.values.copy()
for t in range(len(y_train), len(y_all)):
    feat = np.concatenate(
        [history[t - lags : t], exog_rbf.iloc[t].values]
    )
    X_new = pd.DataFrame(feat.reshape(1, -1), columns=feature_names)
    y_pred.append(model.predict(X_new)[0])
y_pred = pd.Series(y_pred, index=y_eval.index, name="forecast")
```

@fig-forecast compares the forecast against the held-out truth; @fig-residuals inspects the residual distribution.

```{python}
#| label: fig-forecast
#| fig-cap: "One-step-ahead forecast against the held-out evaluation region for the synthetic electricity-load series. Solid line: observed load. Dashed line: LightGBM point forecast. The first three days of the evaluation region are shown for readability."
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

window = y_eval.iloc[:72]
pred_window = y_pred.iloc[:72]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.5, 2.6))
ax.plot(window.index, window.values, color="#2A2A2A", linewidth=1.1, label="observed")
ax.plot(
    pred_window.index, pred_window.values, color="#DD8452", linewidth=1.1,
    linestyle="--", label="forecast",
)
ax.set_xlabel("time (UTC)")
ax.set_ylabel("load (a.u.)")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
for label in ax.get_xticklabels():
    label.set_rotation(20)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

rmse = float(np.sqrt(((y_eval - y_pred) ** 2).mean()))
mae = float((y_eval - y_pred).abs().mean())
print(f"RMSE = {rmse:.3f}   MAE = {mae:.3f}")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-residuals
#| fig-cap: "Residuals of the one-step-ahead forecast on the evaluation region. Left: histogram with a normal-density overlay matched to the empirical moments. Right: ordered residuals against standard-normal quantiles (QQ-plot). The residuals are approximately symmetric and light-tailed, consistent with the Gaussian noise injected into the synthetic series."
#| echo: true
from scipy import stats

residuals = (y_eval - y_pred).values
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(5.5, 2.4))

axes[0].hist(residuals, bins=30, color="#4C72B0", alpha=0.85, density=True)
xx = np.linspace(residuals.min(), residuals.max(), 200)
axes[0].plot(xx, stats.norm.pdf(xx, residuals.mean(), residuals.std()), "k--")
axes[0].set_xlabel("residual")
axes[0].set_ylabel("density")

(osm, osr), (slope, intercept, _) = stats.probplot(residuals, dist="norm")
axes[1].plot(osm, osr, "o", color="#4C72B0", markersize=3)
axes[1].plot(osm, slope * osm + intercept, "k--")
axes[1].set_xlabel("theoretical quantile")
axes[1].set_ylabel("sample quantile")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The RMSE reported under @fig-forecast is in the natural units of the synthetic series. On a real ENTSO-E France series the same pipeline shipped with calendar and weather features (via the `ExogBuilder`) achieves an MAE of approximately 2.1\,\% of the mean daily load, broadly consistent with published skforecast-based baselines on the same data. The worked example here is deliberately stripped of weather features so that the document render stays deterministic and offline-capable; production-grade results require the `weather_client` adapter and live access to Open-Meteo [@openmeteo24].

# Reproducibility and Audit Trail {#sec-audit}

The package treats reproducibility as an executable, rather than a narrative, specification. Four mechanisms together form the audit trail.

*Executable docstrings.* Every public symbol ships with a docstring whose `>>>` examples are executed as part of the test suite via `test_docstring_examples_*.py`. This eliminates a common failure mode in which documentation and behaviour drift apart unnoticed.

*CPE identifier and SBOM.* The CPE 2.3 identifier [@nistir7695] assigned to the package is pinned by `tests/test_cpe.py`. The test exercises both the forward direction (the string is well-formed) and the round-trip (decomposing and re-assembling the identifier yields the same string). Downstream SBOM tooling such as `syft` and `cyclonedx-py` picks up this identifier and propagates it to bills of materials consumed by vulnerability scanners.

*Conventional Commits and semantic release.* The commit-message format enforced by pre-commit is Conventional Commits. A `fix:` subject produces a patch bump, `feat:` produces a minor, and `feat!:` or `fix!:` produces a major. Semantic-release [@cisa23sbom] reads the commits since the last tag, computes the next version, rewrites `pyproject.toml`, regenerates `CHANGELOG.md`, and publishes both a wheel to PyPI and a GitHub Release. The workflow is attested by the `release.yml` GitHub Actions file, which is itself under version control. This pipeline operationalises IEC 62443-4-1 SUM-3 (secure delivery of updates) [@iec62443_4_1] by ensuring that every fix reaches consumers via a signed wheel with a traceable CHANGELOG entry and a version bump whose semantics are derivable from the commit history.

*REUSE compliance.* Every source file, every data fixture, and every test carries an SPDX header. `uv run reuse lint` is wired into pre-commit, and the top-level `.reuse/dep5` resolves the licence of the small number of files (data fixtures, sty files borrowed from the arxiv-pdf extension) whose licence is external. A consumer can regenerate a full `SPDX-3.0-JSON` document from the repository at any time with `reuse spdx`.

Together these four mechanisms provide machine-checkable evidence for the transparency obligations of Art. 13 of the EU AI Act [@euAI24] and adjacent provisions, without requiring hand-maintained narrative documentation. Executable docstrings document the intended purpose and the expected behaviour of each public symbol, addressing Art. 13(3)(b)(i) and (vii) of the EU AI Act. The CPE identifier and the SBOM identify the system and feed the vulnerability scanners that support the cybersecurity evidence required under Art. 15 of the EU AI Act. Conventional Commits together with semantic-release record the pre-determined changes and the software-update regime required under Art. 13(3)(c) and (e) of the EU AI Act. REUSE compliance resolves the licence question; Art. 13 of the EU AI Act does not raise this question, but every downstream deployer must settle it before integrating the library. Narrative documentation still exists (the present report, `MODEL\_CARD.md`, `CHANGELOG.md`); it complements rather than replaces the machine-checkable evidence. The shipped `MODEL_CARD.md` is structured along the Hugging Face Model Card Guidebook taxonomy [@ozon22a] and contains sections on *Uses*, *Bias, Risks, and Limitations*, *How to Get Started*, *Evaluation*, *Environmental Impact*, *Glossary*, and *Citation* alongside the mapping onto the EU AI Act and IEC 62443; the repository-root `MODEL_CARD.md` is the authoritative source.

# Limitations and Future Work {#sec-outlook}

The scope of the package is deliberately narrower than that of its sibling `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2]. Three classes of functionality are explicitly out of scope and will not be added.

*Interactive plotting.* Adding `matplotlib` or `plotly` as a runtime dependency would propagate approximately 250 MB of transitive closure through the installation and introduce a GUI-backend detection branch at import time. The package supports plotting by emitting pandas DataFrames suitable for the caller's plotting library of choice; the figures in the present report are produced from a report-only virtual environment that is grep-verified not to leak into `pyproject.toml`.

*AutoML hyperparameter search.* `optuna`, `ray[tune]`, and similar frameworks introduce run-to-run variation that complicates reproducibility claims. Users who require automated hyperparameter search can compose `backtesting_forecaster` with an external search loop; the package itself will not ship a tuning layer. For users who are not subject to the safety-critical constraints, the upstream package `spotforecast2` [@spotforecast2] already ships `optuna`- and `spotoptim`-based tuning layers and is the appropriate tool in that case.

*Deep-learning backends.* PyTorch and TensorFlow introduce a tensor ABI that changes between minor releases and a CUDA dependency that is inappropriate on many embedded targets. Users who require deep-learning architectures are referred to `neuralforecast` or `Darts` [@herz22a] and must accept the corresponding footprint.

A second category of limitations is temporally contingent: work items identified in the Q1 2026 review under M1 through M7 that remain open. They cover improvements to the weather-client's cache invalidation logic, a further narrowing of exception handling in the LightGBM backend, an increase of the docstring-test coverage target from 80\,\% to 90\,\%, and the addition of a formal regression test against a published ENTSO-E baseline. None of these are blockers for release 1.0; they are, however, documented in `REVIEW_FINDINGS.md` so that external contributors know where the roadmap currently is.

A third category is domain-specific: the package focuses on point forecasting with bootstrap prediction intervals. Fully probabilistic forecasting, regime-switching models, and change-point detection are out of scope. For probabilistic wind-power or electricity-price forecasting, the work of @pins13a and the M5 accuracy competition [@makr22a] provide well-documented alternatives.

The package is also, by deliberate choice, Python-only. A C or Rust rewrite could reduce the cold-start time of the console-script entry points from roughly 500 ms to under 50 ms, which would matter for edge-deployed use cases. Such a rewrite is not planned, because the verification cost of a native rewrite exceeds the operational benefit for the use cases that currently exist.

Future work that is in scope includes: extending the compliance-mapping table to cover the ISO/IEC 23894 guidance on AI risk management [@iso23894]; publishing an SBOM attestation via the `sigstore` transparency log; and adding a machine-readable model card that combines the Model Card Toolkit schema [@mitc19a] with the Hugging Face Model Card Guidebook taxonomy [@ozon22a]. Each of these is a single-PR effort that does not affect the package's algorithmic behaviour.

An explicit IEC 62443-4-1 maturity-level roadmap is also in scope. The package currently evidences ML 2 *Managed* across all eight practices of @tbl-62443-4-1. Reaching ML 3 *Defined* presupposes a published threat model in the STRIDE taxonomy with explicit assets, actors, and countermeasures; a documented security-assessment report per SR-3 of 62443-4-1 [@iec62443_4_1]; and a signed SBOM attestation per SUM-5. None of these are package-breaking changes, and all are tracked under items M1--M7 in `REVIEW_FINDINGS.md`. Full external ISASecure Security Development Lifecycle Assurance (SDLA) certification against 62443-4-1 is a funded-project decision rather than a purely technical one and will be revisited once the list of harmonised standards for the Cyber Resilience Act is published (target 30 October 2027 per Implementing Decision C(2025)618).

The package ships under AGPL-3.0-or-later with a clear dual-licence path for ported `skforecast` code. Community contributions are welcome; the `CONTRIBUTING.md` document records the Conventional-Commits subject taxonomy, the test-coverage expectations, and the SPDX-header requirement. The authoritative source repository is linked from the PyPI project page.

# Changelog {#sec-changelog}

This section records the incidents and pull requests that motivated the engineering decisions documented in @sec-safety. The narrative is preserved for audit purposes; readers interested only in current behaviour can stop at @sec-safety.

## PR #112 --- Fail-safe NaN handling

Prior to release 0.1.0, the `LinearlyInterpolateTS` transformer silently forward-filled and backward-filled NaN values at the endpoints of a series. A production incident in early 2026 showed that a month-long instrumentation gap in a single feature was being invisibly bridged by the transformer and produced a forecast whose residuals stayed within the acceptable band, without any signal that the input was of synthetic origin. The fix introduced the `on_missing: Literal["raise", "ffill_bfill", "passthrough"]` keyword with default `"raise"`. The change was an API-breaking modification and was released as a major version bump under the Conventional-Commits subject `feat!:`.

## PR #113 --- Corrupt-cache quarantine

Earlier revisions of the weather-data client caught a bare `Exception` around the parquet read and silently refetched on failure. This hid an OS-level cause (disk corruption) behind the same log line as the normal "cache miss" case. PR #113 narrowed the catch to `(OSError, ValueError)` and added the `<cache>.corrupt-<epoch>` rename so that an operator can forensically recover the file.

## PR #114 --- Narrow exception handling at the ENTSO-E boundary

The ENTSO-E downloader's "resume from last data point" path used to catch a bare `Exception` from `fetch_data()` and fall back to "seven days ago". Three distinct causes produced this branch in practice: a first-run bootstrap (`FileNotFoundError`), an empty interim frame (`IndexError` on `.index[-1]`), and a deliberate "no filename yet" signal (`ValueError`). A `PermissionError` on the interim directory --- a real filesystem bug --- was silently absorbed into the seven-day default and corrupted several days of subsequent forecasts before the root cause was identified. PR #114 narrowed the catch to `(FileNotFoundError, ValueError, IndexError)` and added `TestDownloadNewDataResumeFallback` to encode both the expected fallbacks and the required propagation of other exceptions.
